cataloging_subject.label: Lincoln Diocese Documents, 1450–1544 (seventy-six-documentary-group selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Andrew Clark's editorially assembled 1914 selection of seventy-six pre-Reformation documentary groups from Lincoln diocesan registers and consistory-court books: sixty-three groups selected under F. J. Furnivall's direction and thirteen additional groups supplied by C. W. Foster. The unit is not one medieval authored work, not every English or quasi-English entry in the registers, not the complete contents of any physical register, and not necessarily seventy-six independent physical documents. The print edition, working transcripts and collation, archival witnesses, U-M encoding, and repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Lincoln Diocese Documents, 1450–1544 (seventy-six-documentary-group selection)
catalog_summary.author: Numerous named and anonymous testators, correspondents, petitioners, ecclesiastical and royal officials, institutions, and documentary clerks
catalog_summary.editor: Andrew Clark; selection and arrangement begun by Frederick J. Furnivall; transcripts and collation supplied by C. W. Foster and an unnamed copyist
catalog_summary.date: Documentary groups 1450–1544; edited selection first published 1914
catalog_summary.region: The historic Diocese of Lincoln and connected English localities; one Paris eyewitness letter

identifiers.scheme: Early English Text Society Original Series

notes: The supplied qualifier makes the exact selection boundary visible and prevents the plural edition title from being read as a complete corpus of Lincoln diocesan documents for 1450–1544.

agents.name: Numerous named and anonymous documentary creators
notes: A cataloging collective for the testators, declarants, correspondents, petitioners, contracting parties, ecclesiastical and royal officials, institutions, and clerks represented in the seventy-six groups; it is not a claim of joint authorship or uniform documentary responsibility.

agents.name: Andrew Clark
notes: Andrew Clark (1856–1922), editor of the 1914 volume, author of its framing and notes, and integrator of the sixty-three earlier transcripts with Foster's thirteen additional documents.

agents.name: Frederick James Furnivall
notes: Frederick James Furnivall (1825–1910) inspected the registers, selected the original sixty-three groups, commissioned their transcription, annotated their subject matter, and arranged them chronologically.

agents.name: Charles Wilmer Foster
notes: Collated doubtful readings in the original sixty-three transcripts against the registers, supplied corrections and information, replaced a lost portion of the John Asserby transcript, and furnished accurate copies of thirteen additional documentary groups.

scope.description: Historical documentary acts and texts represented by the seventy-six numbered edition groups

notes: The schema role author is used broadly for documentary creation. Item-level roles may instead be testator, declarant, issuer, sender, petitioner, contracting party, scribe, registrar, or court official.

scope.description: The 1914 edited selection and its editorial presentation
Integration of Furnivall's sixty-three-document sequence with Foster's thirteen additional documents and the volume's apparatus
Prefatory note, forewords, English summaries of Latin proceedings, contextual introductions, headings, grammar notes, additional notes, and indexes

notes: The U-M encoding omits the main forewords, grammar notes, additional notes, indexes, and textual notes, while retaining contextual prose within the encoded document sequence and appendix.

scope.description: Selection, commissioning, annotation, and chronological arrangement of the original sixty-three-document sequence

notes: Furnivall personally inspected the registers but set an unnamed copyist to transcribe them; he is not mislabeled as the copyist.

responsibilities.display_name: Unnamed copyist commissioned by Frederick J. Furnivall

scope.description: Working transcripts of the original sixty-three groups, except that a lost portion of the John Asserby inventory transcript was replaced by Foster
Accurate copies of all thirteen appendix groups and replacement of the lost portion of main document XXIII
A multitude of doubtful places in the original sixty-three transcripts, checked against their manuscript sources
Dates assigned by Clark to the historical acts and documentary groups selected for the volume

notes: This is the selected documentary range, not a date for every entry in the source registers and not one work-composition event.

scope.description: Furnivall's earlier selection and arrangement followed by Clark's and Foster's preparation of the final seventy-six-documentary-group volume

notes: Clark calls the sixty-three transcripts old but does not date their original preparation precisely.

scope.description: First publication of Clark's EETS edited selection

places.label: Diocese of Lincoln (pre-1541 extent)
notes: The selected records range across the historically extensive medieval and early Tudor diocese; the label is not restricted to the modern county of Lincolnshire.

scope.description: Political, legal, ecclesiastical, and linguistic setting of the selected records
Jurisdictional and archival context of the episcopal-register and consistory-court records

notes: Individual records concern places across several historic counties; the diocesan association is not a claim that each document was composed in Lincoln.

scope.description: Appendix IV, an English eyewitness letter describing persecution and procession in Paris in 1535
Fifteenth-century and linguistically Middle English vernacular passages in wills, codicils, vows, letters, petitions, court statements, and other selected records
Sixteenth-century vernacular documents and passages, especially the Atwater and Longland materials extending through 1544

assertion.notes: The corpus collection's Middle English framing and the XML header's Latin-only declaration are both too broad for the selected vernacular material spanning 1450–1544.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Formal ecclesiastical-court narratives, probate and administrative formulae, complete Latin documents, and Latin portions paired with English codicils or summaries

notes: The XML header declares only Latin, but direct inspection shows that Latin is not the sole or primary language of the selected documentary text.

language_statements.label: English

scope.description: Clark's title, framing, headings, source notes, contextual commentary, summaries of Latin passages, grammar notes, additional notes, and indexes

notes: Modern editorial English is distinct from the historical Middle English documentary passages.

form_statements.description: A prose edited documentary collection. The XML's only two L elements encode Clark's incidental quotation of two lines from Robert Burns within contextual commentary and do not change the substantive documentary form.

scope.description: Ecclesiastical-court proceedings, probate records, commissions, statutes, petitions, agreements, leases, inventories, vows, and related administrative instruments
The many wills, codicils, probate records, and related testamentary materials that dominate the selection
Records enrolled through episcopal and consistory-court jurisdiction in the Diocese of Lincoln
Vows, intercessory practice, Lollardy and magic proceedings, transubstantiation controversy, royal supremacy, festival reform, preaching, and religious persecution

subject_statements.label: Property, landholding, leases, and estate management

summaries.value: Clark's EETS volume assembles seventy-six documentary groups dated 1450–1544 from official registers of the bishops of Lincoln and two consistory-court will books. Wills and probate materials predominate, alongside vows of celibacy, letters, petitions, ecclesiastical-court proceedings, inventories, agreements, statutes, commissions, leases of prebends and estates, and royal or episcopal orders. The selection records property and inheritance, household goods and occupations, burial and intercession, clerical discipline, heresy and religious reform, ecclesiastical jurisdiction, local government, bridges, hospitals, colleges, and estate management across the historically extensive Diocese of Lincoln and connected places.

The 1914 volume contains title and imprint matter, a prefatory note and contents; Forewords on pp. 1–36; sixty-three numbered documents on pp. 37–242; appendix introductions and thirteen Additional Documents on pp. 243–270; Grammar Notes on pp. 271–341; Additional Notes on pp. 342–344; and four indexes on pp. 345–382. The pinned U-M-derived XML retains the printed title page, the sixty-three main groups, the appendix introductions, and the thirteen additional groups on printed pp. 37–270. It omits the prefatory note, contents, main Forewords, Grammar Notes, Additional Notes, indexes, and textual footnotes, notwithstanding the broad title wording 'edited, with notes and indexes.'

The seventy-six numbers are Clark's edition groups, not a count of independent works, archival volumes, original instruments, or even necessarily discrete register entries. Individual groups may contain wills and codicils, summaries and confirmations, proceedings and articles, or abbreviated and full components. Furnivall selected the original sixty-three after personally examining the registers but delegated transcription to an unnamed copyist; Clark explicitly says he never inspected the registers, while Foster collated doubtful passages and supplied the thirteen appendix transcripts. The current record therefore preserves working transcripts and collation as material intermediaries and does not flatten the transmission into direct Clark-to-register transcription.

extent.description: The cataloged edited selection has seventy-six numbered prose documentary groups: sixty-three in the main sequence and thirteen in the appendix. The repository XML contains two BODY DIV1 sections, 76 DIV2 containers, 156 DIV3 containers, 1,485 P elements, 234 PB elements, 156 MILESTONE elements, and two L elements. The two L elements are an incidental Robert Burns quotation in Clark's contextual commentary and are excluded from substantive form metrics. DIV2 and DIV3 counts describe edition and encoding structure, not physical-document totals.

content_parts.label: Title matter, prefatory note, and contents
content_parts.summary: Bibliographic title and imprint matter, Clark's acknowledgment of the seventy-six-documentary-group scope and contributors, and the printed contents table.

locators.value: half-title, series title, title page, Prefatory Note pp. v–vi, and Contents
TEXT/FRONT/TITLEPAGE only; prefatory note and contents omitted

content_parts.label: Forewords
content_parts.summary: Clark's explanation of Furnivall's selection and transcription process, Foster's collation, the documents' court and language context, the participating bishops, and thematic surveys of wills and other record types.
notes: This main Forewords section is deliberately omitted from the U-M encoding and repository XML.

content_parts.label: Sixty-three Lincoln Diocese Documents, 1450–1544
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's chronologically arranged main sequence of sixty-three documentary groups, transcribed by an unnamed copyist and corrected at doubtful places through Foster's manuscript collation.
notes: The sixty-three DIV2 groups contain 134 nested DIV3 units, reflecting composite documentary structure rather than 134 additional cataloged works.
The two XML verse-line elements occur in Clark's contextual quotation within document VIII, not in the underlying testamentary record.

content_parts.label: Appendix: Thirteen Additional Documents
content_parts.summary: Thirteen documentary groups supplied in accurate transcript by Foster, preceded by Clark's explanation of the additions and the Longland Register of Memoranda and divided by a further additional note before Appendix VI.
notes: The appendix has 22 nested DIV3 units. Its introductory prose is retained despite the XML editorial declaration's broad statement that forewords were omitted.

content_parts.label: Grammar Notes
content_parts.summary: Clark's grammatical and linguistic apparatus for the selected documents.
notes: Omitted from the U-M encoding and repository XML.

content_parts.label: Additional Notes
content_parts.summary: Short concluding editorial notes following the grammar section.
notes: Omitted from the U-M encoding and repository XML.

content_parts.label: Indexes of places, persons, matters, and words
content_parts.summary: Four concluding indexes supplying geographic, personal, topical, and lexical access to the edition.

locators.value: Index I, Places, pp. 345–349; Index II, Persons, pp. 350–352; Index III, Matters, pp. 353–363; Index IV, Words, pp. 364–382

notes: All four indexes are present in the complete cached edition scan but omitted from the U-M encoding and repository XML.

evidence.summary: Confirms two BODY DIV1 sections, 76 DIV2, 156 DIV3, 1,485 P, 234 PB, 156 MILESTONE, and two incidental editorial L elements; verifies main and appendix node ranges and the printed page-milestone span [37]–270.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Andrew Clark, ed., Lincoln Diocese Documents, 1450–1544, EETS Original Series 149 (1914).
evidence.summary: Supports the edition boundary, title, bibliography, full contents, documentary topics, editorial responsibilities, transcript history, omitted matter, and first-publication date.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Clark's source notes, working transcripts, Foster's collation, and the Lincolnshire Archives witness crosswalk.
evidence.summary: Supports the eight modeled witness groups, part-scoped source relations, archival custody, and the distinction between edition numbers, transcript intermediaries, and physical register entries.

evidence.summary: Supports the persistent identifier, current public presentation, current raw XML availability, and the distinction between the current transformed XML and the pinned repository artifact.

open_questions.question: How do Clark's seventy-six numbered groups map to distinct register entries, documentary acts, original instruments, enrolled copies, summaries, confirmations, and nested components?
next_steps: Inspect complete images for every cited register and consistory-book locus, recording physical entry boundaries, hands, endorsements, seals or attachments, blank and neighboring leaves, and relationships among wills, codicils, probate acts, summaries, confirmations, and later enrollments.
Build a many-to-many item crosswalk without equating a DIV2, printed Roman number, or nested DIV3 with one independent physical document.

open_questions.question: What are the exact item-level creators, scribes, documentary roles, languages, dates, and places for each component of the seventy-six groups?
next_steps: Describe each component from complete register images and the full Clark apparatus, separately recording creator, declarant, issuer, sender, petitioner, contracting party, scribe, registrar, language, date, place, and documentary state.
Do not propagate one named party, language, date, or place across a composite group without part-level evidence.

open_questions.question: What is the correct Longland-register foliation for main document LXIII when Clark's caption says 229v–230v but the internal printed folio marks and chronology indicate 299v–300v, with the XML inheriting conflicting values?
next_steps: Inspect DIOC/REG/26 around both ff. 229v–230v and 299v–300v against the lease and confirmation printed on pp. 238–242; treat ff. 299v–300v as strongly supported but not yet witness-confirmed.
Check Furnivall's working transcript and Foster's refoliation notes if they survive; preserve both printed claims until direct witness inspection resolves the conflict.

notes: Andrew Clark is not treated as author of the historical documents merely because the U-M title statement places his name in an author-like position.
The sixty-three-plus-thirteen boundary is editorial and evidenced; it must remain explicit in discovery labels and downstream summaries.
The current Lincolnshire Archives record maps the two so-called Lumley documents to the sede-vacante portion of composite DIOC/REG/19, after Lumley's death, rather than to his own episcopal section.

No publicly accessible complete manuscript facsimile of the eight source-witness groups was located in a dated 2026-07-15 check. The complete cached PDF is Clark's 1914 printed edition, not a facsimile of any register.
